entities.name: Inner mitochondrial membrane
entities.description: The folded lipid bilayer that separates the matrix from the intermembrane space and is impermeable to protons except through ATP synthase.

entities.name: Mitochondrial matrix
entities.description: The compartment enclosed by the inner membrane. Hosts the TCA cycle and contains the F1 head of ATP synthase.

entities.name: Intermembrane space
entities.description: The narrow compartment between the outer and inner membranes. Where protons accumulate.

entities.name: Electron transport chain
entities.description: Complexes I, III and IV embedded in the inner membrane. They use the energy of electron transfers (from NADH/FADH2 to O2) to pump protons from matrix to intermembrane space.

entities.name: NADH
entities.description: Electron carrier that delivers high-energy electrons to Complex I.

entities.name: O2
entities.description: The terminal electron acceptor at Complex IV. Without O2 the chain backs up and proton pumping stops.

entities.name: Proton (H+)
entities.description: The currency of the proton-motive force. Pumped out of the matrix by the ETC, flows back through ATP synthase.

entities.name: Proton-motive force
entities.description: The electrochemical gradient across the inner membrane: a chemical (pH) and electrical (membrane potential) difference combined.

entities.name: ATP synthase
entities.description: A two-part rotary machine. F0 sits in the membrane and rotates as protons flow through it. F1 sits in the matrix; rotation drives conformational changes in three catalytic sites that bind ADP and Pi and release ATP.

entities.name: ADP
entities.description: Substrate for ATP synthase. Binds along with Pi at the catalytic sites.

entities.name: ATP
entities.description: Output of the synthase. Carries free energy to the rest of the cell.

entities.name: Inorganic phosphate (Pi)
entities.description: The other substrate. Combined with ADP at the catalytic site to form ATP.

processes.name: Electron transport and proton pumping
processes.description: Electrons from NADH (and FADH2) move down a chain of redox centres. The free energy released at Complexes I, III and IV is used to pump protons from the matrix into the intermembrane space.

processes.name: Establishment of proton-motive force
processes.description: Pumped protons accumulate in the intermembrane space. The result is a combined chemical and electrical gradient — the proton-motive force.

processes.name: Proton-driven rotation of F0
processes.description: Protons re-enter the matrix through the c-ring of F0. Each proton crossing rotates the rotor by one step.

processes.name: ATP synthesis at F1
processes.description: Rotation of the central stalk forces conformational changes in the three catalytic β-subunits of F1. Each subunit cycles through open, loose and tight states (binding-change mechanism), binding ADP+Pi and releasing ATP.

causal_edges.note: Impermeability to protons is what allows the gradient to exist.

prerequisites.concept: Membranes are selectively permeable lipid bilayers.
prerequisites.why: Without an impermeable membrane, no proton gradient can be maintained.

prerequisites.concept: Free-energy difference can drive coupled chemical reactions.
prerequisites.why: ATP synthesis is endergonic on its own; it is paid for by the exergonic flow of protons down their gradient.

prerequisites.concept: Redox reactions move electrons from higher-energy to lower-energy carriers.
prerequisites.why: The ETC is a controlled cascade of redox steps.

misconceptions.wrong: ATP synthase makes ATP directly from electrons.
misconceptions.right: Electrons never enter ATP synthase. The ETC uses electron energy to pump protons; ATP synthase uses proton flow to make ATP.
misconceptions.why_it_sticks: Textbook diagrams compress the chain and the synthase into one ribbon, hiding the energy-conversion step.

misconceptions.wrong: The proton gradient is just a pH difference.
misconceptions.right: It is both chemical (pH) and electrical (membrane potential). The electrical part typically contributes more to the total free energy in mammalian mitochondria.
misconceptions.why_it_sticks: Introductory courses often show only the H+ concentration arrow.

misconceptions.wrong: Each ATP is made by one proton.
misconceptions.right: Each full rotation makes 3 ATP and requires roughly 8 protons (the c-ring stoichiometry varies by species).
misconceptions.why_it_sticks: It is tempting to picture a 1:1 input-output for any molecular machine.

misconceptions.wrong: If oxygen runs out, ATP synthase keeps working as long as the gradient lasts.
misconceptions.right: Briefly true, but with no electrons reaching O2 the ETC stops pumping; the gradient collapses within seconds and synthesis stops.
misconceptions.why_it_sticks: The synthase and the chain look like independent boxes.

confidence_notes.reason: It varies between species (8–15). The mammalian value is ~8.

confidence_notes.claim: The electrical component dominates the proton-motive force.
confidence_notes.reason: True for mammalian mitochondria under typical conditions; not universal.

provenance.note: Hand-curated reference concept map for the vertical slice.
